content_of_post_to_comment:
I realized I was the problem at 2:47 AM, staring at my laptop and doing absolutely nothing.

It was 2:47 AM.I was sitting in my room,laptop open, notes ready, deadline close.

And I was just… not doing anything.

Not tired.Not busy.Just stuck in that weird loop where you open 10 tabs and still avoid the one thing that matters.

Then it hit me in a very uncomfortable way:

Nobody is going to come and fix this moment for me.Not my university.Not my mood.Not some future version of me.

Just me.And I was clearly not doing the job.

That’s when something shifted.

I stopped calling it “lack of motivation” and started calling it what it actually was: avoidance.

Next day I tried something different.

I did the smallest possible version of the work I was avoiding.Just 10 minutes . No hero arc.Just action.

And something stupid happened.

It worked.

Not instantly.Not dramatically.But enough to break the pattern.

So I kept going:

1.when I didn’t feel like studying, I studied badly but still studied

2.when I wanted to delay everything to “tomorrow”, I forced myself into 5–10 minute starts

3.when I caught myself overthinking, I just started before I was ready

Life didn’t magically become easy.

But I stopped being surprised that it was hard.

Now I still procrastinate sometimes.I still negotiate with myself like I’m two different people.

But the difference is: one of them actually does the work now.

And that changed everything.

If you’re waiting for motivation, clarity, or the “right mindset”… I was too.

Turns out they don’t show up first.

Action does.
comment: The 2:47 AM moment is painfully relatable because avoidance often feels like being lazy when it’s really just discomfort with starting. I’ve noticed the 5-minute rule works because it removes the pressure to suddenly become a different person overnight. The hardest part is usually just breaking the agreement you made with tomorrow-you.
